posts.title: Making the Web a Better Place: Steve Krug's Usability Wisdom
posts.description: Learn Steve Krug's essential usability principles from 'Don't Make Me Think!' - how to stop frustrating your visitors.
posts.content:
Have you ever visited a website looking for something simple—like a store's phone number or a product price—only to find yourself trapped in a digital maze of pop-ups, confusing menus, and dead ends?

This is what Steve Krug set out to eliminate in his classic book, Don't Make Me Think! First published in 2000, this book offers a practical, common-sense approach to creating websites that people actually enjoy using.

Krug's central message is simple: good usability is invisible. When a website works well, users don't notice the design—they simply accomplish what they came to do.

Don't Make Me Think!

This is Krug's First Law of Usability. The goal is simple: when someone looks at your webpage, it should be self-evident. They should understand what the page is and what they can do without consciously thinking about it.

But on the web, we constantly create tiny question marks in users' heads:

  • Using cute names for navigation links (calling a jobs page "Job-o-Rama" instead of just "Jobs")
  • Hiding the search bar behind a tiny magnifying glass icon
  • Making buttons that don't look like buttons
  • Using industry jargon that only insiders understand

Each micro-frustration drains the user's patience, pushing them closer to the back button.

How We Really Use the Web

Here's a truth that might hurt: most designers build websites as if users are going to read every word. The reality? Users don't read websites—they scan them.

Krug identifies three key behaviors:

1. We Scan, Not Read

We look for scent—visual clues that tell us we're on the right track. Headlines, bold text, and bullet points act like signposts.

2. We Satisfice

We don't weigh all options to find the absolute best choice. We click the first reasonable-looking option because the penalty for being wrong is just hitting the Back button.

3. We Muddle Through

We don't read instructions. We forge ahead clicking things and seeing what happens. If our mental model is wrong, we get frustrated with the website.

Billboard Design 101

Users whiz by your site like a car going 60 mph. You have seconds to communicate your message. Five key strategies:

  • Clear Visual Hierarchy: Most important elements should be most prominent
  • Use Conventions: Don't reinvent the wheel. Logo goes top-left, search goes top-right
  • Define Areas: Break pages into clearly defined zones
  • Make Clickables Obvious: Buttons should look like buttons
  • Minimize Noise: Remove decorative elements that don't serve a purpose

Animal, Vegetable, or Mineral?

Krug's Second Law: It doesn't matter how many times I have to click, as long as each click is a mindless, unambiguous choice.

Three clear clicks are infinitely better than one confusing click. Don't make users think about which option to choose.

Omit Needless Words

Krug's Third Law: Get rid of half the words on each page, then get rid of half of what's left.

Kill the happy talk ("Welcome to our website!"). Remove instructions (if you need them, your design failed). Cut redundancy.

Before: "In order to submit your application for the open position, please click on the button below..."
After: "Click Submit."

Navigation IS the Website

The web has no physical sense of scale. Without good navigation, users are lost in digital space. Every page needs:

  • Site ID: Your logo in the top-left (clickable to home)
  • Page Name: Clear name matching what the user clicked
  • You Are Here: Visual indicator showing current section
  • Breadcrumbs: Show the path (Home > Products > Electronics)

Usability Testing on 10 Cents a Day

Testing one user is 100% better than testing none. You don't need a fancy lab. Test 3-4 users once a month. Grab a coworker, show them your site, and watch where they get confused.

The Reservoir of Goodwill

Every user enters with a limited supply of patience. Every frustrating experience drains it:

  • Confusing navigation? There goes goodwill.
  • Hidden prices? More goodwill down the drain.
  • Forced registration? Goodwill evaporating fast.

When the reservoir hits zero, users leave and don't come back.

Conclusion

If you remember only one thing: Don't make your users think.

Make things obvious. Use clear labels. Create visual hierarchy. Test with real people. Build a website that works—not one that wins design awards, but one that simply works.

Now, go simplify something!

posts.title: Mastering CSS Grid and Flexbox
posts.description: A deep dive into modern CSS layout techniques that will change how you design web interfaces.
posts.content:
CSS has evolved dramatically. Today we have powerful tools like Grid and Flexbox that make complex layouts simple.

Flexbox for One-Dimensional Layouts

Use Flexbox when aligning items in a row or column:

  • Navigation bars
  • Card components
  • Centering elements

Grid for Two-Dimensional Layouts

Use CSS Grid when controlling both rows and columns:

  • Page layouts
  • Photo galleries
  • Dashboard interfaces

Understanding when to use each tool is key to maintainable CSS.

posts.title: The Future of Web Development
posts.description: Exploring emerging trends in 2026: AI-assisted coding, WebAssembly, and the new era of static sites.
posts.content:
The web development landscape is shifting rapidly.

AI-Assisted Development

Tools like GitHub Copilot have evolved into genuine pair programmers. They're not replacing developers—they're amplifying our capabilities.

WebAssembly Maturation

WASM is becoming a target for high-performance web applications, from video editing to CAD software.

The Static Site Renaissance

With GitHub Pages, Netlify, and Vercel, static sites are experiencing a renaissance. We get fast initial loads and rich interactivity.
